mnemonics: BFFS: Bigger, Faster, Fuller Space. To make an algorithm faster (F), you often need to use a bigger (B), fuller (F) data structure that consumes more Space (S).
keyPoints: A space-time tradeoff is a situation where the memory use (space) of an algorithm can be reduced at the cost of slower execution time (time), and vice versa.
Precomputation (e.g., pre-sorting data, lookup tables, memoization) is a common technique that uses extra space to save time during execution.
Choosing the right data structure (e.g., a hashtable for fast lookups vs. a list for compact storage) is a direct application of this principle.
commonMistakes: Optimizing for time when space is the critical constraint (or vice-versa), leading to an inefficient solution for the problem's context.
Over-optimizing prematurely without profiling, often resulting in complex code for negligible gain.
Ignoring the constant factors hidden by Big-O notation; a theoretically faster algorithm with a large space overhead might be slower in practice for the expected input size.
